text: Hey Surgical YouTubers, it's Kelly Slater here, the surgeon not the surfer. I'm a general surgeon in Brisbane, Australia. Today we're going to be talking about how I do a simple laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

Please remember this video is for educational purposes only and if you're a patient, you must talk to your surgeon about your diagnosis and treatment. If you're a doctor, you must hold suitable qualifications to carry out this procedure.
First, let's take a look at the anatomy.
Upon initiating laparoscopy, the gallbladder becomes visible as a retractor is placed on its fundus and pushed towards the diaphragm, revealing a straightforward anatomical presentation.
The surgeon identifies the cystic node and cystic artery within Calot's triangle, followed by the cystic duct and common bile duct.
The surgeon identifies Rivia sulcus, which contains the inflow to the posterior segments of the right hemiliver and the base of segment four.

In an easy gallbladder and more importantly a difficult gallbladder you need to stay superior to Rivia sulcus at all times and also well to the right of the base of segment four in order to avoid a common bile duct injury.
The common bile duct is clearly visible at the base of segment four, with the surgical field oriented to the right to avoid injury.
The surgeon identifies the anterior right hepatic artery running anterior to the common bile duct, with the cystic artery visibly branching directly from it.

The surgeon uses minimal diathermy to incise the peritoneum on the left side of the gallbladder during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The left hand holds the retractor on Hartman's pouch, maintaining tension and control over the gallbladder to ensure effective cauterization.
If you don't do this you're wasting movements and time and this can be really tiring if you are doing a long case.
The surgeon uses a Maryland Grasper to dissect tissue around the gallbladder, demonstrating quick and atraumatic manipulation of the structures.

The surgeon uses the Maryland Grasper in parallel to the cystic artery and cystic duct, positioning it behind the cystic artery while the left hand maneuvers to stabilize and retract surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a grasper to dissect behind the cystic artery in one spread, effectively completing the dissection. The cystic artery is divided early to open up Calot's triangle.

The surgeon carefully examines the vessel to ensure it is the cystic artery and not the larger looping right hepatic artery, exercising caution due to the size of the vessel before proceeding with division.
The surgeon identifies the cystic node as a marker for the cystic artery, placing one clip low and another high on the artery to maximize its length before division.

The surgeon divides the cystic artery near the top clip, ensuring stability and preventing clip displacement. The dissection remains high on the cystic duct, allowing for potential further dissection if additional length is needed.

The surgeon uses diathermy to clean the section between the cystic duct and the liver within the clearly visible Calot's triangle. Strands are released to unravel the spiral valves of the cystic duct. The gallbladder is partially dissected off the liver to ensure a clear critical view, confirming the identification of the cystic duct.

The surgeon clips a posterior cystic artery, which is visible and considered too large for electrocautery, ensuring clearance from a cystic duct that may originate from a right posterior sectoral duct.

The surgeon holds Hartman's pouch firmly while manipulating the cystic duct, which is visible entering the pouch. The left hand is used to stabilize the area without repeatedly grasping, minimizing the risk of creating holes in the tissue.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection with a Maryland retractor to further dissect the cystic duct, pulling parallel to the duct. A small amount of bleeding occurs but is expected to stop quickly, with no diathermy applied to the cystic duct.
The surgeon clips the distal end of the cystic duct in preparation for performing a cholangiogram, ensuring the duct is securely closed for imaging.

The surgeon inspects the bile duct for stones and checks the anatomy to ensure no injury to the common bile duct. Using the left hand, the surgeon cuts the cystic duct at an angle suitable for the cholangiogram catheter.
The surgeon massages the proximal cystic duct to check for embedded stones, then introduces the Olsen-Reddick cannula into the duct.

The surgeon palms the instrument while attempting to place the catheter into the cystic duct. The catheter encounters resistance from the spiral valves within the duct. The surgeon uses a Maryland retractor to try to open the cystic valves, but the catheter insertion remains challenging.

The surgeon uses a Maryland retractor to attempt dilation of the cystic duct, aiming to break the spiral valve. After unsuccessful attempts, the surgeon prepares to make a cut lower on the cystic duct, below one of the valves, to facilitate catheter insertion.

The surgeon squeezes the cystic duct to check for stones, noting the return of bile, indicating a valve obstruction. An incision is made lower on the tiny cystic duct to facilitate catheter insertion, successfully achieving a clean cholangiogram.

The surgeon removes the Olsen-Reddick cannula after achieving the cholangiogram and places two clips across the cystic duct, ensuring they are securely positioned. The surgeon carefully checks the placement of the clips before completing the cut through the cystic duct.

The surgeon uses diathermy to divide extra tissue, ensuring the hook moves upwards towards the abdominal wall to prevent thermal injury. The gallbladder remains attached to the liver by its peritoneal attachment. The liver is pulled upwards towards the diaphragm to create a line of tension.

The surgeon uses the heel of the diaphragm to apply tension and facilitate the removal of the gallbladder from the liver bed. Any bleeding from the cystic veins is cauterized. The gallbladder is then placed in a retrieval bag to prevent spillage of stones into the abdomen.

The surgeon irrigates the abdominal cavity in small amounts, focusing on Rutherford Morrison's pouch to avoid fat obstruction. The area is carefully suctioned to remove all bile and any spilled stones, ensuring the gallbladder bed is completely dry.
The surgeon withdraws the laparoscopic ports, checks for any bleeding in the surgical field, and proceeds to close the incisions.
